reason: 198.x = Class C -> default /24; 255.255.255.252 = /30 -> 6 borrowed bits = 2^6 = 64 subnets; 2 host bits -> 2^2-2 = 2 hosts.
Retaining data without power is the definition of non-volatile storage.
Permanent start-up memory that survives power off is ROM (BIOS/firmware); RAM is volatile.
DRAM stores bits in leaky capacitors and must be periodically refreshed; SRAM/ROM/EPROM do not.
USB flash drive is secondary (auxiliary) storage, not primary/main memory; 'Axillary' is a misspelling and Secondary is the standard term.
Standard data hierarchy: character -> field -> record -> file -> database; D wrongly puts Record before Field.
Floppy, tape and network drive can all serve as backup targets -> All of the above.
4 bits = half a byte = nibble.
Main memory (RAM) is internal/primary memory, as opposed to external/auxiliary secondary storage.
unanswerable - stem is garbled ('contents of memory into blocks of the same size'); the concept it points at is paging/frames, and none of ROM/EPROM/EEPROM/All-of-above is a correct answer.
Permanent built-in memory = ROM; CD-ROM is removable media, not built in.
A plain CD (CD-ROM) is a read-only medium; genuinely ambiguous since CD-R/CD-RW are writable, so 'read and write' is defensible.
RAM is the main (primary) memory; 'internal' is also true, so the item has two defensible options but 'main' is the intended term.
Permanent memory built into the computer is ROM.
Tape must be wound past intervening data, i.e. sequential access, so random recall is slow.
Core memory retains state without power (non-volatile); it is bulkier, costlier, and its reads ARE destructive (requiring rewrite), which rules out A, B and C.
Magnetic hard disks retain data without power -> non-volatile; 'Flash' describes SSDs, not HDDs.

Flash is non-volatile electrically-erasable ROM-family memory, so ROM is the best of the readable options; option E ('E REM') is garbled and may be intended as EEPROM, which would be the better answer. No source key.
Unchangeable start-up instructions (bootstrap/BIOS) live in ROM.
Flash memory is commonly called flash RAM in this question-bank tradition, though 'flash ROM' is arguably more accurate (flash is an EEPROM variant) - the item is ambiguous.
